title: Corporate Comebacks: How Boeing, Intel, and Macy's Are Rebuilding
content:
After several rough years, Fortune 500 stalwarts Boeing, Intel, and Macy's are writing promising new chapters. Here's how they're turning things around.

No major company lasts for long without a losing streak or two. The three featured here have each succeeded for long enough to earn corporate-icon status. But they've also recently endured crises serious enough to qualify as existential threats. These include fatal crashes and quality defects involving Boeing's 737 Max; Intel's failure to keep up in the global semiconductor race; and Macy's overexposure to the declining American mall in the age of Amazon.

The good news is that they have all turned a corner. In their comeback efforts, each company is tapping the energy of a new CEO. Furthermore, each is strengthening the commitment to innovation that helped it join the Fortune 500 in the first place.

When Boeing went looking for a CEO in 2024, the airplane maker was in crisis. It was an iconic American manufacturer in danger of forever losing its luster. Just as Boeing was recovering from the reputational damage of two 737 Max jet crashes, a door-plug blowout in 2024 trained a harsh spotlight on its manufacturing practices. Its defense and space division was also booking multibillion-dollar losses. The eventual pick, Kelly Ortberg, was a strategic choice with a strong track record, offering a new path forward for the troubled company.
